narrative: As a Black male applicant with a strong LSAT score of 39—above the typical 36.7 average—and a family income in the high bracket, you brought several promising elements to your law school application. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 was close to the average of 3.21, and planning to attend full-time aligns with the vast majority of students at 92.7%. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 48.7%, tipping the decision toward rejection to prioritize students with lower predicted risks. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school grade decile of 1, well below the average of 5.53, signaling to the model a significant dip in later academic performance compared to peers. This stood out as the top driver, outweighing other strengths. Your LSAT score was the strongest counterbalance, actively pulling the prediction toward success by demonstrating exceptional standardized reasoning ability relative to most applicants. Family income provided a moderate positive nudge too, as higher brackets often correlate with resources that support bar passage in the model's view. Overall, while your test scores and background suggested potential, the low Year 3 decile dominated, making your profile riskier than typical admitted students who maintain stronger consistent rankings. This doesn't reflect your full potential or personal story, but rather how these patterns played out in past bar outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking courses or building recent academic momentum to boost that decile, perhaps through post-baccalaureate programs or targeted prep that showcases upward trends. Strengthening any weaker areas with concrete evidence of improvement could reshape how models like this evaluate you next time. Many applicants in similar situations have succeeded by addressing key gaps head-on, and with your LSAT edge, you're well-positioned to retry stronger.
